mappings.rationaleAI: Cross-cultural analysis of ritual encompassing rites of passage, sacrifice, birth, marriage, and death ceremonies directly addresses religious rites and customs (A), rituals of worship (A.1), passage and purification rites (A.2), and religious feasts and festivals (A.4).
Cross-cultural study of pilgrimage spanning sacred sites including Mecca, Lourdes, Canterbury, and the Ganges directly addresses pilgrimage (A.1.c) as a ritual of worship.

Monasticism spanning Christian and Buddhist traditions with communal organization, rules of conduct, and hierarchical governance directly addresses monasticism (A.3.c) and forms of religious organization (B.2).

Cross-cultural analysis of sainthood and veneration from the early Christian Church through the modern era directly addresses sainthood (B.3). Saints as artistic and literary subjects relate to art and architecture, religious symbolism (C.2).

Martyrdom across Christianity, Islam, and other traditions, including how martyrs are constructed through cultural memory, relates to sainthood and the veneration of those who sacrificed their lives for faith (B.3).
Druids as a priestly caste practicing prophecy, sacrifice (A.1.d), and divination (A.3.f) represent a form of religious organization (B.2).
The Bible as sacred texts central to Judaism and Christianity, covering composition, transmission, and canonization, directly addresses sacred writings (C.1).
The Quran as the central sacred text of Islam, covering its role as physical codex, liturgical text, and scriptural authority, directly addresses sacred writings (C.1).
The Old Testament encompassing the Hebrew Bible and various Christian canons directly addresses sacred writings (C.1).
The New Testament as early Christian writings covering canon formation directly addresses sacred writings (C.1).
Literary analysis of the Hebrew Bible relates to sacred writings (C.1).
The Dead Sea Scrolls as manuscripts significant for biblical canon formation relate to sacred writings (C.1).
Non-canonical early Christian texts relate to sacred writings (C.1).
The Book of Mormon as a sacred text relates to sacred writings (C.1).
The Book of Common Prayer as a liturgical text shaping worship relates to rituals of worship (A.1), sacred writings (C.1), and ceremonial objects and sacraments (C.3).
Christian art with liturgical functions and religious iconography directly addresses art and architecture, religious symbolism and iconography (C.2).
Jewish religious practice including prayer (A.1.a), dietary laws (A.3.b), festivals (A.4), and synagogue organization (B.2). The Torah and Talmud relate to sacred writings (C.1).
Indigenous African religious traditions covering rites of passage (A.2), festivals (A.4), divination (A.3.f), and religious institutions (B.2).
Islam's Sharia relates to religious law (A.3.a), the caliphate to religious states (B.1), and mosque organization to B.2.
Islamic jurisprudence directly addresses religious law (A.3.a).
Anglican liturgy and church governance relate to rituals of worship (A.1) and forms of religious organization (B.2).
Vatican II's impact on liturgy, sacraments, and organization relates to rituals of worship (A.1), forms of religious organization (B.2), and sacraments (C.3).
Catholic Reformation's institutional renewal relates to forms of religious organization (B.2), religious education (B.4), and art (C.2).
Secularism contrasts with theocracies and religious states (B.1).
Contemplative practices relate to prayer (A.1.a) and asceticism (A.3.e).
Quaker silent worship relates to rituals of worship (A.1) and religious organization (B.2).
Christianity's institutional forms relate to religious organization (B.2) and sacred writings (C.1).
Blasphemy relates to religious regulation of behavior (A.3).
Polygamy relates to passage rites (A.2) and religious regulation (A.3).
Just war theory relates to religious regulation of behavior (A.3).
Covers sacred texts, sacraments, and prayer across major world religions — directly relevant to sacred writings and ceremonial objects.

Covers Catholic sacraments, art, sacred architecture, organizational structure, and missionary history — directly relevant to rituals of worship, religious organization, art and architecture, and ceremonial objects.
Covers reshaping of church-state relations, vernacular Bible translation, and religious pluralism — relevant to forms of religious organization and sacred writings.

Covers fashion as cultural communication including identity construction — the only candidate with potential relevance to religious dress and vestments (unresolved leaf C.4) through its examination of dress as cultural signifier, though the connection is moderate.
